stem: Find the smallest positive solution \( x \) (in radians) of \( \cos x=\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \).
solution: Since \( \arccos \) returns a value in \( [0,\pi] \), and this is the smallest nonnegative angle with the given cosine (the next solution, \( 2\pi-\arccos\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \), is larger), the smallest positive solution is \( x=\arccos\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives a negative angle rather than the smallest POSITIVE solution.
This uses the reflection rule for sine instead of the direct arccos value for cosine.
This adds a full extra period, overshooting the smallest solution.
This uses the OTHER solution in \( [0,2\pi) \), which is larger, not the smallest one.
This adds an extra \( \pi \) to the correct smallest solution.
This subtracts an extra \( \pi \), producing a non-positive value.

stem: Find the smallest positive solution \( x \) (in radians) of \( \sin x=\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \).
solution: Since \( 0<\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}<1 \), \( \arcsin\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \) lies in \( \left(0,\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \); \( \sin \) is increasing there, and the only other solution in \( [0,2\pi) \), namely \( \pi-\arcsin\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]} \), is larger. So the smallest positive solution is \( x=\arcsin\dfrac{[[a]]}{[[b]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the OTHER solution in \( [0,2\pi) \) (the second-quadrant angle), which is larger, not the smallest one.
This gives a negative angle rather than the smallest POSITIVE solution.
This adds a full extra period, overshooting the smallest solution.
This uses the wrong quadrant reflection entirely.
This adds an extra \( \pi \) to the correct smallest solution.
This subtracts an extra \( \pi \), producing a non-positive value.

stem: Find the smallest positive solution \( x \) (in radians) of \( [[A]]\tan x=[[B]] \).
solution: Dividing gives \( \tan x=\dfrac{[[B]]}{[[A]]}>0 \), so \( x=\arctan\dfrac{[[B]]}{[[A]]} \) lies in \( \left(0,\tfrac{\pi}{2}\right) \); since \( \tan \) has period \( \pi \), this is the smallest positive solution, \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio \( B/A\) before taking the arctangent.
This gives a negative angle rather than the smallest POSITIVE solution.
This uses the sine/cosine reflection rule instead of tangent's period-\( \pi \) shift.
This adds a full extra period, overshooting the smallest solution.
This subtracts a full period, producing a non-positive value.
This adds an extra \( \pi \) on top of a sign flip.

stem: How many solutions does \( \sin([[k]]x)=\dfrac{[[c_num]]}{[[c_den]]} \) have for \( x \) in \( [0,2\pi) \)?
solution: Substituting \( \theta=[[k]]x \), as \( x \) ranges over \( [0,2\pi) \), \( \theta \) ranges over \( [0,2([[k]])\pi) \), which is exactly \( [[k]] \) full periods of \( \sin\theta \). Since \( \dfrac{[[c_num]]}{[[c_den]]}\ne\pm1 \), each period contributes exactly \( 2 \) solutions, for a total of \( 2\cdot[[k]]=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts only \( 1 \) solution per period instead of \( 2 \).
This counts \( 4 \) solutions per period instead of \( 2 \).
This overcounts by one solution.
This undercounts by one solution.
This forgets that each period of \( \sin\theta=c\) (with \( -1<c<1 \)) contributes two solutions, not one.
This overcounts by two solutions.
